• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Bayesian Logistic Regression for Low Muscle Mass Prediction (NHANES 2011–2018)

CIND860 Capstone Project — Toronto Metropolitan University

Adiel Mallik

June 17, 2026

1 Data Loading and Preparation

This report presents a Bayesian logistic regression analysis complementing the machine-learning models fitted in the companion report. The Bayesian framework offers calibrated uncertainty quantification, interpretable posterior distributions for each predictor, and formal prior sensitivity analysis — none of which are readily available from penalised frequentist or ensemble methods.

The same cleaned NHANES 2011–2018 dataset is used. The outcome low_muscle_mass is a binary indicator (1 = ASMI below the sex-specific 20th percentile; 0 = normal).

# ── Load dataset ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA_PATH <- "nhanes_clean_2011_2018.xlsx"   # must be in the same folder

nhanes_raw <- read_excel(DATA_PATH) %>%
  mutate(
    low_muscle_mass = as.integer(low_muscle_mass),
    sex             = factor(sex, levels = c("Male", "Female")),
    race_ethnicity  = factor(race_ethnicity)
  )

cat("Raw dataset:", nrow(nhanes_raw), "rows ×", ncol(nhanes_raw), "cols\n")
## Raw dataset: 14347 rows × 22 cols
# ── Select predictors and restrict to complete cases ──────────────────────────
# Predictors specified in the analysis plan
PRED_VARS <- c("age_years", "sex", "race_ethnicity",
               "bmi", "protein_g", "poverty_ratio",
               "waist_cm", "max_grip_kg")

OUTCOME   <- "low_muscle_mass"

nhanes_cc <- nhanes_raw %>%
  select(all_of(c(PRED_VARS, OUTCOME))) %>%
  filter(!is.na(low_muscle_mass)) %>%
  drop_na()

cat("Complete-case rows:", nrow(nhanes_cc), "\n")
## Complete-case rows: 4992
cat("Outcome prevalence:", round(mean(nhanes_cc$low_muscle_mass) * 100, 1), "%\n")
## Outcome prevalence: 21.4 %
# Prevalence by sex
nhanes_cc %>%
  group_by(sex) %>%
  summarise(n = n(),
            n_lmm = sum(low_muscle_mass),
            pct   = round(mean(low_muscle_mass) * 100, 1),
            .groups = "drop") %>%
  kable(caption = "Low-muscle-mass prevalence by sex (complete cases)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Low-muscle-mass prevalence by sex (complete cases)
sex n n_lmm pct
Male 2552 535 21.0
Female 2440 535 21.9 
# ── Standardise continuous predictors ─────────────────────────────────────────
# brms works best with standardised continuous inputs; it stabilises sampling
# and makes priors on coefficients directly comparable across predictors.
CONT_VARS <- c("age_years", "bmi", "protein_g", "poverty_ratio",
               "waist_cm", "max_grip_kg")

nhanes_scaled <- nhanes_cc %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(CONT_VARS), ~ as.numeric(scale(.))))

# ── Stratified 70/30 train/test split ─────────────────────────────────────────
set.seed(42)
train_idx  <- createDataPartition(nhanes_scaled$low_muscle_mass,
                                  p = 0.70, list = FALSE)
train_df   <- nhanes_scaled[ train_idx, ]
test_df    <- nhanes_scaled[-train_idx, ]

cat("Train:", nrow(train_df), "| Test:", nrow(test_df), "\n")
## Train: 3495 | Test: 1497
cat("Train prevalence:", round(mean(train_df$low_muscle_mass) * 100, 1), "%\n")
## Train prevalence: 20.9 %
cat("Test  prevalence:", round(mean(test_df$low_muscle_mass)  * 100, 1), "%\n")
## Test  prevalence: 22.6 %

The 20 % outcome prevalence is consistent with the by-design definition (bottom sex-specific quintile of ASMI). All continuous predictors are mean-centred and scaled to unit standard deviation prior to modelling so that coefficient priors are on the same scale.

2 Bayesian Logistic Regression

2.1 Prior Specification

We adopt weakly informative priors following Gelman et al. (2008):

Parameter Prior Rationale
Intercept Normal(−1.39, 0.5) log-odds of 20 % prevalence = log(0.2/0.8) ≈ −1.39
All coefficients Normal(0, 0.5) Regularising; allows OR up to ~e^1 ≈ 2.7 per SD

The intercept prior encodes our strong expectation that roughly 20 % of adults have low muscle mass (because the outcome is defined by the 20th percentile), while the coefficient priors are weakly informative — they discourage implausibly large effects without dominating the likelihood.

# ── Primary model formula ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
bayes_formula <- bf(
  low_muscle_mass ~ age_years + sex + race_ethnicity +
                    bmi + protein_g + poverty_ratio +
                    waist_cm + max_grip_kg,
  family = bernoulli(link = "logit")
)

# ── Prior specification ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
priors_main <- c(
  prior(normal(-1.39, 0.5), class = Intercept),
  prior(normal(0, 0.5),     class = b)          # all slope coefficients
)

# ── Fit primary model ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
set.seed(42)
fit_main <- brm(
  formula  = bayes_formula,
  data     = train_df,
  prior    = priors_main,
  chains   = 4,
  iter     = 4000,
  warmup   = 2000,
  seed     = 42,
  cores    = getOption("mc.cores", parallel::detectCores()),
  backend  = "rstan",
  refresh  = 0,
  silent   = 2
)
cat("Primary model fitted successfully.\n")
## Primary model fitted successfully.
summary(fit_main)
##  Family: bernoulli 
##   Links: mu = logit 
## Formula: low_muscle_mass ~ age_years + sex + race_ethnicity + bmi + protein_g + poverty_ratio + waist_cm + max_grip_kg 
##    Data: train_df (Number of observations: 3495) 
##   Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 4000; warmup = 2000; thin = 1;
##          total post-warmup draws = 8000
## 
## Regression Coefficients:
##                                 Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat
## Intercept                          -2.60      0.18    -2.95    -2.26 1.00
## age_years                           0.19      0.07     0.06     0.32 1.00
## sexFemale                          -1.31      0.19    -1.69    -0.96 1.00
## race_ethnicityNonMHispanicAsian     0.26      0.19    -0.11     0.63 1.00
## race_ethnicityNonMHispanicBlack    -1.25      0.21    -1.66    -0.82 1.00
## race_ethnicityNonMHispanicWhite    -0.03      0.17    -0.35     0.30 1.00
## race_ethnicityOtherHispanic         0.08      0.23    -0.37     0.53 1.00
## race_ethnicityOtherDMultiracial    -0.34      0.29    -0.90     0.21 1.00
## bmi                                -4.68      0.23    -5.13    -4.23 1.00
## protein_g                          -0.06      0.06    -0.19     0.06 1.00
## poverty_ratio                      -0.16      0.06    -0.28    -0.04 1.00
## waist_cm                            1.02      0.18     0.66     1.37 1.00
## max_grip_kg                        -1.00      0.11    -1.21    -0.80 1.00
##                                 Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS
## Intercept                           5223     5346
## age_years                           7180     5795
## sexFemale                           5474     5705
## race_ethnicityNonMHispanicAsian     4722     5000
## race_ethnicityNonMHispanicBlack     5642     6034
## race_ethnicityNonMHispanicWhite     4385     5197
## race_ethnicityOtherHispanic         5859     5915
## race_ethnicityOtherDMultiracial     8133     6147
## bmi                                 4589     5277
## protein_g                           9800     6275
## poverty_ratio                       9140     5690
## waist_cm                            5028     5375
## max_grip_kg                         5359     5588
## 
## Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS). For each parameter, Bulk_ESS
## and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential
## scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).

3 Prior Sensitivity Analysis

We refit the model under two alternative prior specifications to assess how sensitive the posterior inferences are to the choice of priors:

  • (a) Flat priors — improper flat priors on all slope coefficients (no b class prior specified, so Stan places no regularisation on them); only the intercept prior Normal(−1.39, 0.5) is retained. Useful as a reference maximum-likelihood-like estimate but not recommended for small subgroups.
  • (b) Conservative priors — Normal(0, 0.25) on coefficients, encoding the belief that most predictors have modest effects (OR < e^0.5 ≈ 1.65 per SD).
# ── (a) Flat priors on coefficients ──────────────────────────────────────────
# In brms, truly flat (improper) priors on class = b are obtained by specifying
# NO prior for that class at all. We keep only the intercept prior so the chain
# remains anchored to the known ~20% prevalence, while all slopes are estimated
# without any regularisation. This is the "reference" specification.
priors_flat <- c(
  prior(normal(-1.39, 0.5), class = Intercept)
  # class = b intentionally omitted → Stan uses an improper flat prior
)

set.seed(42)
fit_flat <- brm(
  formula = bayes_formula,
  data    = train_df,
  prior   = priors_flat,
  chains  = 4,
  iter    = 4000,
  warmup  = 2000,
  seed    = 42,
  cores   = getOption("mc.cores", parallel::detectCores()),
  backend = "rstan",
  refresh = 0,
  silent  = 2
)
# ── (b) Conservative priors: Normal(0, 0.25) ──────────────────────────────────
priors_cons <- c(
  prior(normal(-1.39, 0.5), class = Intercept),
  prior(normal(0, 0.25),    class = b)
)

set.seed(42)
fit_cons <- brm(
  formula  = bayes_formula,
  data     = train_df,
  prior    = priors_cons,
  chains   = 4,
  iter     = 4000,
  warmup   = 2000,
  seed     = 42,
  cores    = getOption("mc.cores", parallel::detectCores()),
  backend  = "rstan",
  refresh  = 0,
  silent   = 2
)
# ── Extract posterior medians and 95% CIs for all three fits ──────────────────
extract_post <- function(fit, label) {
  post <- as_draws_df(fit)
  # Keep only b_ columns (slopes) and the intercept
  b_cols <- grep("^b_", names(post), value = TRUE)
  post_long <- post %>%
    select(all_of(b_cols)) %>%
    pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "parameter", values_to = "draw") %>%
    group_by(parameter) %>%
    summarise(
      median = round(median(draw), 4),
      q2.5   = round(quantile(draw, 0.025), 4),
      q97.5  = round(quantile(draw, 0.975), 4),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    mutate(
      CI_95   = paste0("[", q2.5, ", ", q97.5, "]"),
      Prior   = label,
      # Clean up parameter names
      parameter = gsub("^b_", "", parameter),
      parameter = gsub("race_ethnicity", "race:", parameter)
    ) %>%
    select(Prior, parameter, median, CI_95)
}

sens_main <- extract_post(fit_main, "Main (N(0,0.5))")
sens_flat <- extract_post(fit_flat, "Flat")
sens_cons <- extract_post(fit_cons, "Conservative (N(0,0.25))")

sens_tbl <- bind_rows(sens_main, sens_flat, sens_cons) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Prior,
              values_from = c(median, CI_95),
              names_glue  = "{Prior}_{.value}") %>%
  arrange(parameter)

# Re-order columns sensibly
col_order <- c("parameter",
               "Main (N(0,0.5))_median", "Main (N(0,0.5))_CI_95",
               "Flat_median",            "Flat_CI_95",
               "Conservative (N(0,0.25))_median", "Conservative (N(0,0.25))_CI_95")
col_order <- col_order[col_order %in% names(sens_tbl)]
sens_tbl  <- sens_tbl %>% select(all_of(col_order))

sens_tbl %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "Prior Sensitivity Analysis",
    subtitle = "Posterior median and 95% credible interval under three prior specifications"
  ) %>%
  cols_label(
    parameter = "Parameter"
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(label = "Main: Normal(0, 0.5)",
              columns = starts_with("Main")) %>%
  tab_spanner(label = "Flat",
              columns = starts_with("Flat")) %>%
  tab_spanner(label = "Conservative: Normal(0, 0.25)",
              columns = starts_with("Conservative")) %>%
  cols_label(
    .fn = function(x) gsub("^.*_(median|CI_95)$", "\\1", x)
  ) %>%
  tab_footnote(
    "Robust inference: parameters whose CIs exclude zero across all three
     specifications can be considered prior-insensitive."
  )
Prior Sensitivity Analysis
Posterior median and 95% credible interval under three prior specifications
Parameter
Main: Normal(0, 0.5)
Flat
Conservative: Normal(0, 0.25)
Main (N(0,0.5))_median Main (N(0,0.5))_CI_95 Flat_median Flat_CI_95 Conservative (N(0,0.25))_median Conservative (N(0,0.25))_CI_95
Intercept -2.6025 [-2.9514, -2.2623] -2.8772 [-3.3294, -2.4398] -2.3375 [-2.6038, -2.0736]
age_years 0.1923 [0.0622, 0.3204] 0.1478 [0.0064, 0.2876] 0.2157 [0.1049, 0.3284]
bmi -4.6801 [-5.1297, -4.2318] -6.7104 [-7.4001, -6.0658] -3.0112 [-3.3214, -2.7088]
max_grip_kg -0.9990 [-1.2138, -0.7999] -1.2010 [-1.4547, -0.9485] -0.7692 [-0.9401, -0.601]
poverty_ratio -0.1623 [-0.2806, -0.0449] -0.1683 [-0.297, -0.0385] -0.1420 [-0.2503, -0.0329]
protein_g -0.0604 [-0.1895, 0.0626] -0.0904 [-0.227, 0.0408] -0.0354 [-0.1502, 0.076]
race:NonMHispanicAsian 0.2548 [-0.1051, 0.6311] -0.0077 [-0.4733, 0.4787] 0.3421 [0.0599, 0.6073]
race:NonMHispanicBlack -1.2458 [-1.6605, -0.8248] -1.7792 [-2.3308, -1.2428] -0.8582 [-1.154, -0.5671]
race:NonMHispanicWhite -0.0325 [-0.3519, 0.2988] -0.3933 [-0.8124, 0.0428] 0.1203 [-0.125, 0.3629]
race:OtherDMultiracial -0.3323 [-0.8996, 0.2107] -0.8527 [-1.6703, -0.0486] -0.1249 [-0.5181, 0.2591]
race:OtherHispanic 0.0769 [-0.3673, 0.5268] -0.0928 [-0.6608, 0.4704] 0.0758 [-0.2442, 0.394]
sexFemale -1.3113 [-1.6887, -0.9551] -1.5137 [-1.947, -1.0756] -0.9793 [-1.2565, -0.6945]
waist_cm 1.0152 [0.6641, 1.373] 2.2617 [1.7889, 2.7576] 0.0364 [-0.2296, 0.3003]
Robust inference: parameters whose CIs exclude zero across all three specifications can be considered prior-insensitive.

Parameters whose credible intervals exclude zero consistently across all three prior specifications provide the strongest evidence for a genuine association with low muscle mass, irrespective of the specific regularisation chosen.

4 Posterior Diagnostics

Markov chain convergence is assessed via the R-hat statistic (Gelman & Rubin, 1992; values < 1.01 indicate convergence) and the bulk and tail effective sample sizes (Vehtari et al., 2021; values > 400 per chain are adequate). Trace plots provide visual confirmation of good mixing.

# ── Extract R-hat and ESS for all parameters ──────────────────────────────────
diag_df <- as_draws_df(fit_main) %>%
  summarise_draws() %>%
  filter(grepl("^b_|^Intercept", variable)) %>%
  mutate(
    variable  = gsub("^b_", "", variable),
    variable  = gsub("race_ethnicity", "race:", variable),
    rhat      = round(rhat,     4),
    ess_bulk  = round(ess_bulk, 0),
    ess_tail  = round(ess_tail, 0),
    converged = if_else(rhat < 1.01, "Yes", "No")
  ) %>%
  select(variable, rhat, ess_bulk, ess_tail, converged)

diag_df %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "MCMC Convergence Diagnostics — Primary Model",
    subtitle = "R-hat < 1.01 and ESS > 400 per chain indicate adequate convergence"
  ) %>%
  cols_label(
    variable  = "Parameter",
    rhat      = "R-hat",
    ess_bulk  = "ESS (Bulk)",
    ess_tail  = "ESS (Tail)",
    converged = "Converged?"
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#FFDDDD"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = converged == "No")
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#DDFFDD"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = converged == "Yes")
  ) %>%
  tab_footnote("R-hat values > 1.01 flagged in red; indicate potential non-convergence.")
MCMC Convergence Diagnostics — Primary Model
R-hat < 1.01 and ESS > 400 per chain indicate adequate convergence
Parameter R-hat ESS (Bulk) ESS (Tail) Converged?
Intercept 1.0006 5223 5346 Yes
age_years 1.0005 7180 5795 Yes
sexFemale 1.0003 5474 5705 Yes
race:NonMHispanicAsian 1.0003 4722 5000 Yes
race:NonMHispanicBlack 1.0004 5642 6034 Yes
race:NonMHispanicWhite 1.0006 4385 5197 Yes
race:OtherHispanic 1.0007 5859 5915 Yes
race:OtherDMultiracial 0.9998 8133 6147 Yes
bmi 1.0000 4589 5277 Yes
protein_g 1.0007 9800 6275 Yes
poverty_ratio 1.0016 9140 5690 Yes
waist_cm 1.0004 5028 5375 Yes
max_grip_kg 1.0004 5359 5588 Yes
Intercept 1.0000 6127 5720 Yes
R-hat values > 1.01 flagged in red; indicate potential non-convergence. 
# ── Trace plots for primary predictors ────────────────────────────────────────
# Select non-race parameters + intercept for readability
post_draws <- as_draws_array(fit_main)
key_params <- c("b_Intercept", "b_age_years", "b_sexFemale",
                "b_bmi", "b_waist_cm", "b_max_grip_kg",
                "b_protein_g", "b_poverty_ratio")

# Keep only params that exist in draws
key_params <- key_params[key_params %in% dimnames(post_draws)[[3]]]

mcmc_trace(post_draws, pars = key_params,
           facet_args = list(ncol = 2, labeller = label_parsed)) +
  labs(title    = "MCMC Trace Plots — Primary Model (Key Parameters)",
       subtitle = "Each colour = one chain; horizontal mixing indicates convergence") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11)

Trace plots for key model parameters. Well-mixed chains with no trend indicate convergence. 

mcmc_dens_overlay(post_draws, pars = key_params) +
  labs(title    = "Posterior Density Overlay — Primary Model",
       subtitle = "Overlapping densities across chains confirm good mixing") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 11)

Posterior density plots for key parameters. Overlapping chains confirm convergence.

5 Posterior Summaries and Inference

5.1 Coefficient Summary

For each predictor we report the posterior median (point estimate), 95 % credible interval (CI), the probability of direction (pd; the proportion of the posterior on the same side as the median — equivalent to a one-sided p-value), and the odds ratio (OR = exp(coefficient)) for continuous predictors standardised to one SD.

# ── Posterior summaries ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
post_summary <- describe_posterior(
  fit_main,
  effects     = "fixed",
  component   = "all",
  centrality  = "median",
  ci          = 0.95,
  ci_method   = "HDI",
  test        = c("pd", "rope"),
  rope_range  = c(-0.1, 0.1)   # rope around zero on log-odds scale
)

post_tbl <- post_summary %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  filter(grepl("^b_|Intercept", Parameter)) %>%
  mutate(
    Parameter  = gsub("^b_", "", Parameter),
    Parameter  = gsub("race_ethnicity", "race:", Parameter),
    Median     = round(Median, 4),
    CI_low     = round(CI_low,  4),
    CI_high    = round(CI_high, 4),
    pd         = round(pd,      4),
    OR         = round(exp(Median), 3),
    CI_95      = paste0("[", CI_low, ", ", CI_high, "]"),
    Significant = if_else(CI_low > 0 | CI_high < 0, "Yes", "No")
  ) %>%
  select(Parameter, Median, CI_95, OR, pd, Significant)

post_tbl %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "Posterior Summaries — Primary Bayesian Logistic Regression",
    subtitle = "Outcome: Low Muscle Mass | Predictors standardised (continuous)"
  ) %>%
  cols_label(
    Parameter   = "Predictor",
    Median      = "Posterior Median",
    CI_95       = "95% HDI",
    OR          = "Odds Ratio",
    pd          = "P(Direction)",
    Significant = "95% CI excludes 0?"
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = list(cell_fill(color = "#DDEEFF"),
                     cell_text(weight = "bold")),
    locations = cells_body(rows = Significant == "Yes")
  ) %>%
  tab_footnote(
    "Median and 95% highest density interval (HDI) on the log-odds scale.
     OR = exp(Median). P(Direction) > 0.975 ≈ p < 0.05 two-sided.
     Rows highlighted in blue have 95% HDI fully excluding zero."
  )
Posterior Summaries — Primary Bayesian Logistic Regression

Median and 95% highest density interval (HDI) on the log-odds scale. OR = exp(Median). P(Direction) > 0.975 ≈ p < 0.05 two-sided. Rows highlighted in blue have 95% HDI fully excluding zero.

5.2 Plain-Language Interpretation

# ── Extract key posteriors for narrative ──────────────────────────────────────
get_est <- function(param_pattern) {
  post_tbl %>%
    filter(grepl(param_pattern, Parameter, ignore.case = TRUE)) %>%
    slice(1) %>%
    select(Parameter, Median, OR, CI_95, pd)
}

interp_vars <- c("age_years", "sexFemale", "bmi", "waist_cm",
                 "max_grip_kg", "protein_g", "poverty_ratio")

interp_tbl <- map_dfr(interp_vars, get_est)

kable(interp_tbl,
      col.names = c("Predictor", "Log-Odds Median", "OR", "95% HDI", "P(Direction)"),
      caption = "Key predictor estimates for narrative interpretation") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Key predictor estimates for narrative interpretation
Predictor Log-Odds Median OR 95% HDI P(Direction)
age_years 0.1923 1.212 [0.0566, 0.3129] 0.9972
sexFemale -1.3113 0.269 [-1.6785, -0.9454] 1.0000
bmi -4.6801 0.009 [-5.1039, -4.2128] 1.0000
waist_cm 1.0152 2.760 [0.668, 1.3758] 1.0000
max_grip_kg -0.9990 0.368 [-1.2138, -0.7998] 1.0000
protein_g -0.0604 0.941 [-0.1843, 0.0665] 0.8370
poverty_ratio -0.1623 0.850 [-0.2835, -0.0484] 0.9970

Narrative interpretation of significant predictors:

The results below are based on the posterior medians and 95 % highest density intervals. All continuous predictors are on the standardised (z-score) scale; ORs reflect the change in odds of low muscle mass per one standard deviation increase in the predictor.

# Build narrative dynamically from the posterior table
sig_preds <- post_tbl %>% filter(Significant == "Yes",
                                  !grepl("Intercept", Parameter))

cat("\n")
for (i in seq_len(nrow(sig_preds))) {
  p   <- sig_preds$Parameter[i]
  med <- sig_preds$Median[i]
  OR  <- sig_preds$OR[i]
  ci  <- sig_preds$CI_95[i]
  dir <- if (med > 0) "increases" else "decreases"

  cat(sprintf(
    "- **%s**: A one-SD increase %s the log-odds of low muscle mass by %.3f
      (OR = %.3f; 95%% HDI %s), with a probability of direction of %.4f.\n\n",
    p, dir, abs(med), OR, ci, sig_preds$pd[i]
  ))
}

  • age_years: A one-SD increase increases the log-odds of low muscle mass by 0.192 (OR = 1.212; 95% HDI [0.0566, 0.3129]), with a probability of direction of 0.9972.

  • sexFemale: A one-SD increase decreases the log-odds of low muscle mass by 1.311 (OR = 0.269; 95% HDI [-1.6785, -0.9454]), with a probability of direction of 1.0000.

  • race:NonMHispanicBlack: A one-SD increase decreases the log-odds of low muscle mass by 1.246 (OR = 0.288; 95% HDI [-1.6471, -0.8142]), with a probability of direction of 1.0000.

  • bmi: A one-SD increase decreases the log-odds of low muscle mass by 4.680 (OR = 0.009; 95% HDI [-5.1039, -4.2128]), with a probability of direction of 1.0000.

  • poverty_ratio: A one-SD increase decreases the log-odds of low muscle mass by 0.162 (OR = 0.850; 95% HDI [-0.2835, -0.0484]), with a probability of direction of 0.9970.

  • waist_cm: A one-SD increase increases the log-odds of low muscle mass by 1.015 (OR = 2.760; 95% HDI [0.668, 1.3758]), with a probability of direction of 1.0000.

  • max_grip_kg: A one-SD increase decreases the log-odds of low muscle mass by 0.999 (OR = 0.368; 95% HDI [-1.2138, -0.7998]), with a probability of direction of 1.0000.

6 Classification Performance

6.1 Posterior Predictive Probabilities

We generate posterior predictive draws for each test-set observation, average across all 8,000 post-warmup draws to obtain a predicted probability, then apply a 0.5 classification threshold.

# ── Posterior predictive means for test set ───────────────────────────────────
# posterior_epred returns a draws × n_obs matrix of predicted probabilities
set.seed(42)
ppc_mat  <- posterior_epred(fit_main, newdata = test_df, allow_new_levels = TRUE)

# Average over posterior draws → point probability estimate per observation
pred_prob <- colMeans(ppc_mat)
pred_class_num <- as.integer(pred_prob > 0.5)

truth_01  <- test_df$low_muscle_mass
truth_fac <- factor(ifelse(truth_01 == 1, "LowMuscle", "Normal"),
                    levels = c("Normal", "LowMuscle"))
pred_fac  <- factor(ifelse(pred_class_num == 1, "LowMuscle", "Normal"),
                    levels = c("Normal", "LowMuscle"))

cat("Confusion matrix (Bayesian model, threshold = 0.5):\n")
## Confusion matrix (Bayesian model, threshold = 0.5):
cm_bayes <- confusionMatrix(pred_fac, truth_fac, positive = "LowMuscle")
print(cm_bayes$table)
##            Reference
## Prediction  Normal LowMuscle
##   Normal      1104       109
##   LowMuscle     55       229
# ── Compute classification metrics ────────────────────────────────────────────
roc_bayes <- roc(truth_01, pred_prob, quiet = TRUE)

sens_b <- cm_bayes$byClass["Sensitivity"]
spec_b <- cm_bayes$byClass["Specificity"]
prec_b <- cm_bayes$byClass["Precision"]
rec_b  <- cm_bayes$byClass["Recall"]

# F1 macro
f1_pos   <- ifelse(!is.na(prec_b) & !is.na(rec_b) & (prec_b + rec_b) > 0,
                   2 * prec_b * rec_b / (prec_b + rec_b), NA)
prec_neg <- cm_bayes$byClass["Neg Pred Value"]
rec_neg  <- spec_b
f1_neg   <- ifelse(!is.na(prec_neg) & !is.na(rec_neg) & (prec_neg + rec_neg) > 0,
                   2 * prec_neg * rec_neg / (prec_neg + rec_neg), NA)
f1_macro_b <- mean(c(f1_pos, f1_neg), na.rm = TRUE)

# MCC
tp_b <- as.numeric(cm_bayes$table["LowMuscle","LowMuscle"]); tp_b <- ifelse(is.na(tp_b),0,tp_b)
tn_b <- as.numeric(cm_bayes$table["Normal",   "Normal"   ]); tn_b <- ifelse(is.na(tn_b),0,tn_b)
fp_b <- as.numeric(cm_bayes$table["LowMuscle","Normal"   ]); fp_b <- ifelse(is.na(fp_b),0,fp_b)
fn_b <- as.numeric(cm_bayes$table["Normal",   "LowMuscle"]); fn_b <- ifelse(is.na(fn_b),0,fn_b)
mcc_den_b <- sqrt((tp_b+fp_b)*(tp_b+fn_b)*(tn_b+fp_b)*(tn_b+fn_b))
mcc_b     <- if (is.na(mcc_den_b) || mcc_den_b == 0) 0 else ((tp_b*tn_b)-(fp_b*fn_b))/mcc_den_b

bayes_metrics <- data.frame(
  Model       = "Bayesian Logistic Reg.",
  AUC         = round(as.numeric(auc(roc_bayes)), 4),
  F1_Macro    = round(f1_macro_b, 4),
  MCC         = round(mcc_b,      4),
  Sensitivity = round(sens_b,     4),
  Specificity = round(spec_b,     4)
)

cat("Bayesian model classification metrics:\n")
## Bayesian model classification metrics:
print(bayes_metrics)
##                              Model    AUC F1_Macro    MCC Sensitivity
## Sensitivity Bayesian Logistic Reg. 0.9508   0.8336 0.6719      0.6775
##             Specificity
## Sensitivity      0.9525

6.2 Comparison with ML Models

# ── ML model results extracted from the companion HTML report ─────────────────
# Source: Test-Set Performance of Four Classifiers table,
#         Mallik_CIND860_Capstone_Analysis.html
ml_metrics <- data.frame(
  Model       = c("Elastic Net LR", "Random Forest", "XGBoost", "SVM (Radial)"),
  AUC         = c(0.9571, 0.9411, 0.9477, 0.8640),
  F1_Macro    = c(0.8418, 0.8072, 0.8410, 0.1614),
  MCC         = c(0.7072, 0.6230, 0.6905, -0.4569),
  Sensitivity = c(0.9311, 0.6143, 0.8540, 0.5565),
  Specificity = c(0.8619, 0.9532, 0.8903, 0.0576)
)

# Combine all models
all_metrics <- bind_rows(ml_metrics, bayes_metrics)

all_metrics %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "Full Model Comparison: Bayesian vs. Machine Learning",
    subtitle = "NHANES 2011–2018 | Held-out test set | Outcome: Low Muscle Mass"
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = where(is.numeric), decimals = 4) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = list(cell_fill(color = "#E8F5E9"),
                     cell_text(weight = "bold")),
    locations = cells_body(rows = Model == "Bayesian Logistic Reg.")
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = list(cell_fill(color = "#DDEEFF"),
                     cell_text(weight = "bold")),
    locations = cells_body(rows = Model == "Elastic Net LR")
  ) %>%
  cols_label(
    Model       = "Model",
    AUC         = "AUC",
    F1_Macro    = "F1 (Macro)",
    MCC         = "MCC",
    Sensitivity = "Sensitivity",
    Specificity = "Specificity"
  ) %>%
  tab_footnote(
    "ML model results extracted from companion report (Mallik_CIND860_Capstone_Analysis.html).
     Bayesian model (green) uses posterior predictive means with 0.5 threshold.
     Elastic Net highlighted in blue as the best-performing ML model by AUC."
  ) %>%
  tab_source_note(
    "All models evaluated on the same 30% held-out test partition (seed = 42)."
  )
Full Model Comparison: Bayesian vs. Machine Learning
NHANES 2011–2018 | Held-out test set | Outcome: Low Muscle Mass
Model AUC F1 (Macro) MCC Sensitivity Specificity
Elastic Net LR 0.9571 0.8418 0.7072 0.9311 0.8619
Random Forest 0.9411 0.8072 0.6230 0.6143 0.9532
XGBoost 0.9477 0.8410 0.6905 0.8540 0.8903
SVM (Radial) 0.8640 0.1614 −0.4569 0.5565 0.0576
Bayesian Logistic Reg. 0.9508 0.8336 0.6719 0.6775 0.9525
ML model results extracted from companion report (Mallik_CIND860_Capstone_Analysis.html). Bayesian model (green) uses posterior predictive means with 0.5 threshold. Elastic Net highlighted in blue as the best-performing ML model by AUC.
All models evaluated on the same 30% held-out test partition (seed = 42). 
# ── ROC curve ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
roc_df <- data.frame(
  FPR   = 1 - roc_bayes$specificities,
  TPR   = roc_bayes$sensitivities
)

ggplot(roc_df, aes(x = FPR, y = TPR)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#2196F3", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.6, y = 0.25,
           label = sprintf("AUC = %.4f", as.numeric(auc(roc_bayes))),
           size = 5, colour = "#2196F3", fontface = "bold") +
  labs(title    = "ROC Curve — Bayesian Logistic Regression (Test Set)",
       subtitle = "Posterior predictive mean probabilities | NHANES 2011–2018",
       x = "False Positive Rate (1 – Specificity)",
       y = "True Positive Rate (Sensitivity)") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13)

ROC curve for the Bayesian logistic regression model on the held-out test set

7 Bayes Factors for Key Predictors

Bayes factors (BF) quantify the relative evidence for H1 (the coefficient differs from zero) versus H0 (the coefficient equals zero), computed via the Savage–Dickey density ratio using bayestestR::bayesfactor_parameters().

  • BF > 10: Strong evidence for H1
  • BF 3–10: Moderate evidence for H1
  • BF 1/3–3: Inconclusive
  • BF < 1/3: Evidence for H0 (null effect)

We compute BFs for the five strongest predictors identified from the posterior summary (largest |median| log-odds).

# ── Identify top 5 predictors by |posterior median| ──────────────────────────
top5 <- post_tbl %>%
  filter(!grepl("Intercept|race:", Parameter)) %>%
  mutate(abs_median = abs(Median)) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs_median)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 5) %>%
  pull(Parameter)

cat("Top 5 predictors for Bayes factor analysis:\n")
## Top 5 predictors for Bayes factor analysis:
print(top5)
## [1] "bmi"         "sexFemale"   "waist_cm"    "max_grip_kg" "age_years"
# ── Compute Bayes factors via Savage-Dickey ratio ─────────────────────────────
# Requires refitting with sample_prior = "yes" so the prior is also sampled
# Refit primary model sampling from the prior as well (needed for BF computation)
set.seed(42)
fit_bf <- brm(
  formula      = bayes_formula,
  data         = train_df,
  prior        = priors_main,
  chains       = 4,
  iter         = 4000,
  warmup       = 2000,
  seed         = 42,
  cores        = getOption("mc.cores", parallel::detectCores()),
  sample_prior = "yes",   # ← required for Savage-Dickey BF
  backend      = "rstan",
  refresh      = 0,
  silent       = 2
)
# ── Extract Bayes factors ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
bf_results <- bayesfactor_parameters(fit_bf, null = 0)

# Format for display
bf_tbl <- bf_results %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  filter(grepl("^b_", Parameter)) %>%
  mutate(
    Parameter     = gsub("^b_", "", Parameter),
    Parameter     = gsub("race_ethnicity", "race:", Parameter),
    log_BF        = round(log_BF, 3),
    BF10          = round(exp(log_BF), 2),
    Evidence      = case_when(
      BF10 > 100  ~ "Extreme",
      BF10 > 30   ~ "Very strong",
      BF10 > 10   ~ "Strong",
      BF10 > 3    ~ "Moderate",
      BF10 > 1    ~ "Anecdotal (H1)",
      BF10 > 1/3  ~ "Inconclusive",
      TRUE        ~ "Evidence for H0"
    )
  ) %>%
  # Filter to top 5 predictors
  filter(Parameter %in% top5) %>%
  arrange(desc(BF10)) %>%
  select(Parameter, BF10, log_BF, Evidence)

bf_tbl %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "Bayes Factors for Top 5 Predictors",
    subtitle = "Savage–Dickey density ratio | H1: coefficient ≠ 0 vs. H0: coefficient = 0"
  ) %>%
  cols_label(
    Parameter = "Predictor",
    BF10      = "BF₁₀",
    log_BF    = "log(BF₁₀)",
    Evidence  = "Strength of Evidence"
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(BF10, log_BF), decimals = 2) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = list(cell_fill(color = "#DDEEFF"),
                     cell_text(weight = "bold")),
    locations = cells_body(rows = BF10 > 10)
  ) %>%
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#E8F5E9"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = BF10 > 3 & BF10 <= 10)
  ) %>%
  tab_footnote(
    "BF₁₀ > 10 (strong evidence) highlighted in blue.
     BF₁₀ 3–10 (moderate evidence) highlighted in green.
     Computed via Savage–Dickey density ratio from the joint prior/posterior."
  )
Bayes Factors for Top 5 Predictors
Savage–Dickey density ratio | H1: coefficient ≠ 0 vs. H0: coefficient = 0
Predictor BF₁₀ log(BF₁₀) Strength of Evidence
bmi 29,694,378,985,310,225,820,549,120.00 58.65 Extreme
max_grip_kg 108,254,987.75 18.50 Extreme
sexFemale 693,148.82 13.45 Extreme
waist_cm 23,860.99 10.08 Extreme
age_years 8.48 2.14 Moderate
BF₁₀ > 10 (strong evidence) highlighted in blue. BF₁₀ 3–10 (moderate evidence) highlighted in green. Computed via Savage–Dickey density ratio from the joint prior/posterior. 
bf_tbl %>%
  mutate(Parameter = fct_reorder(Parameter, log_BF)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Parameter, y = log_BF,
             fill = if_else(log_BF > log(10), "Strong", "Moderate/Weak"))) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = log(10), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "#2196F3", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = log(3),  linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "#4CAF50", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Strong" = "#2196F3",
                               "Moderate/Weak" = "#90CAF9")) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.6, y = log(10) + 0.2,
           label = "log(BF=10)", hjust = 0, size = 3.5, colour = "#2196F3") +
  annotate("text", x = 0.6, y = log(3) + 0.2,
           label = "log(BF=3)",  hjust = 0, size = 3.5, colour = "#4CAF50") +
  labs(title  = "Log Bayes Factors — Top 5 Predictors",
       x = NULL, y = "log(BF₁₀)",
       fill = "Evidence Strength") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Log Bayes factors for the top 5 predictors. Values above zero favour H1 (effect present); below zero favour H0 (null effect).
